followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the overall verdict as promising, with a similar score. The other reviews strengthened my confidence that the low-mismatch result is real within scope, but also reinforced my concerns about empirical phase modeling, nuisance-parameter flexibility, non-like-for-like precision comparisons, and the limited strength of the GR-test interpretation. I slightly reduce novelty relative to my first review because the dependence on recent prior same-group greybody work is clearer.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: The claim that Eq. (4) uses a linear b1*omega phase correction appears inconsistent with the rest of the bundle and with the other reviewer's reading of the model as a phi_1/omega correction; this looks like a misread or hallucinated notation.
The very positive assessment that the model simply sidesteps start-time ambiguity is too strong; Appendix D makes clear that a frequency-window choice remains important.
Calling the GW250114 precision comparison well-supported overstates the evidence, because the analysis setup is not fully like-for-like with the comparison posteriors.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The phase correction is empirical or post-hoc rather than derived from the Green's-function argument; this is a real caveat.
The comparison to TEOBPM or other time-domain models needs an identical mismatch definition, frequency band, and optimization setup before supporting a strong superiority claim.
The GW250114 credible-interval comparison is weakened by fixed extrinsic parameters; narrower posteriors may not reflect more physical information.

The test demonstrated in the paper is weaker than an inspiral-versus-ringdown GR test because both GreyRing and QNM spectroscopy use post-merger information, even though they probe different waveform structures.
The reliance on Ref. [111] as a recent same-group theoretical foundation should be treated as a spot-check item rather than accepted as independently settled.

main_claims.claim: A greybody-factor based frequency-domain ansatz can fit the full complex post-merger waveform, extending earlier amplitude-only greybody-factor models.
main_claims.notes: The numerical evidence is strong for the tested regime. However, the phase correction is empirical and source-dependent, so the full complex model is not derived from the greybody factor alone.
main_claims.paper_location: Main text, GreyRing section; Eqs. (2)-(4); Appendix B, GreyRing model

main_claims.claim: GreyRing achieves very low mismatches for the dominant quadrupolar mode over comparable-mass, aligned-spin SXS simulations.
main_claims.notes: This is the paper's best-supported technical result, within the stated nonprecessing/quasicircular SXS scope. The exact numerical level still needs code/configuration reproducibility and a clear training-versus-validation audit.
main_claims.paper_location: Validation against numerical simulations; Fig. 2; Appendix B, Figs. 4-5

main_claims.claim: GreyRing typically outperforms state-of-the-art time-domain ringdown models such as TEOBPM.
main_claims.notes: Other reviewers correctly flagged that the comparison may not be like-for-like: GreyRing uses an unweighted frequency-domain mismatch and imports the comparison to time-domain models from another work rather than recomputing all models under identical choices.
main_claims.paper_location: Validation against numerical simulations; Discussion; comparison to Ref. [52]

main_claims.claim: GreyRing enables a post-merger-only consistency test of GR by inferring remnant mass and spin from the greybody factor and comparing with QNM spectroscopy.
main_claims.notes: The proposed test is conceptually valuable, but not fully agnostic: it assumes a Kerr reflection amplitude and includes phenomenological nuisance parameters that may absorb unmodeled physics or weaken interpretability.
main_claims.paper_location: Tests of gravity with GreyRing section; Appendix C

main_claims.claim: The GW250114 GreyRing posterior for final mass and spin is compatible with standard ringdown and full-signal analyses and slightly narrower than standard black-hole spectroscopy.
main_claims.notes: Compatibility is plausible, but the precision comparison is weakened because several extrinsic parameters are fixed and the setup is not demonstrably identical to the LVK/pyRing analyses.
main_claims.paper_location: Application to GW250114; Fig. 3; Appendix D

main_claims.claim: GreyRing avoids overtone and ringdown-start-time ambiguities.
main_claims.notes: It avoids a literal time-domain start-time choice, but replaces it with a frequency-window choice. Appendix D shows strong cutoff dependence for phenomenological parameters, although final mass/spin are reported to be more stable.
main_claims.paper_location: Tests of gravity with GreyRing; Discussion; Appendix D, Figs. 9-10

missing_assumptions.assumption: The source-dependent phase and amplitude corrections improve fit quality without washing out physically meaningful deviations from Kerr.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is central to interpreting GreyRing as a no-hair or GR test rather than just a flexible phenomenological ringdown fit.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The fixed-extrinsic-parameter treatment in the GW250114 analysis has negligible impact on the final mass/spin intervals under full joint marginalization.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The claim of comparable or better precision than standard spectroscopy may disappear if all relevant extrinsic, calibration, and PSD uncertainties are marginalized consistently.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The chosen frequency window can be selected by a transferable prescription before looking at the answer.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Appendix B fitting range; Appendix D cutoff study
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without an operational event-independent window rule, the method inherits a tuning ambiguity analogous to ringdown start-time selection.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Neglecting mode mixing and nonlinear contributions is harmless for the dominant-mode GW250114 analysis and for the frequency bands used.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Appendix B, Fits to numerical simulations; Discussion
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: These effects could bias remnant parameters or be absorbed into nuisance terms, especially for high spin or high-SNR events.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The unweighted mismatch distribution is a reliable proxy for detector-weighted modeling error in current and next-generation analyses.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Validation section; Discussion on SNR criteria
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The SNR-reach claim depends on translating intrinsic fit quality into parameter-estimation accuracy.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the PDF equations and implementation define the phase correction, reflection-amplitude phase branch, and Fourier-convention signs consistently across the full sampled frequency range?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The HTML extraction drops several displayed formulas, and the conflicting reviewer descriptions of the phase term make direct inspection of the PDF/source or code necessary.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the SXS simulations used for validation independent of any simulations used to select, tune, or calibrate the GreyRing ansatz and prior amplitude model of Ref. [113]?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The text describes catalog selection but does not fully separate model-selection, fitting, and validation datasets.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Would the GW250114 mass/spin posterior remain as tight after full marginalization over sky location, distance, inclination, polarization, calibration, PSD uncertainty, and reasonable priors on all nuisance parameters?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires rerunning the Bayesian analysis, not just reading the manuscript.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the comparison with TEOBPM/Ref. [52] based on exactly the same waveform preprocessing, mismatch definition, frequency interval, and optimization over extrinsic degrees of freedom?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The needed external configurations and code are not included in the bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How large are nonlinear mode and spherical-spheroidal mixing biases for the dominant mode at GW250114-like spin and SNR?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper acknowledges these effects but does not quantify their contribution to the presented inference.

novelty_assessment: The novelty remains meaningful but somewhat less sweeping after the other reviews. Earlier greybody-factor amplitude modeling and the immediately preceding same-group Green's-function work carry part of the conceptual load. The new contribution is the full complex GreyRing template, especially the phase-complete empirical ansatz, and its Bayesian use as a post-merger consistency test. That is a strong practical advance, but closer to a promising proof-of-concept framework than a finished first-principles precision test.
score_rationale: GreyRing is a strong and interesting proof of concept with impressive empirical fit quality in the tested aligned-spin SXS regime and a timely application to GW250114. I do not see a fatal flaw. The score stays at 7 because the core result is promising, but technical soundness is capped by unresolved nuisance-parameter interpretability, fixed-extrinsic real-data inference, unweighted mismatch extrapolations, and incomplete reproducibility. Novelty is high enough to matter, though less than field-shaping because it builds directly on recent same-group greybody-factor work.
summary: GreyRing is a promising frequency-domain ringdown model using the Kerr reflection amplitude/greybody factor plus phenomenological amplitude and phase corrections. The paper gives strong empirical evidence for low mismatches on aligned-spin, comparable-mass SXS waveforms and a useful proof-of-concept GW250114 analysis. After reading the other reviews, I keep my prior view: the contribution is interesting and likely worth surfacing, but the strongest claims about precision, robustness, and GR-testing power remain more weakly established than the mismatch plots alone suggest.

technical_objections.evidence: Appendix D fixes sky location, inclination, polarization, and distance to Table 2 values, while allowing coalescence time and phase to vary or be marginalized. The main text then compares the resulting GreyRing credible regions with standard LVK/pyRing ringdown analyses and emphasizes slightly narrower posteriors.
technical_objections.objection: The GW250114 precision claim is not established under a like-for-like inference setup because several extrinsic parameters are fixed rather than fully marginalized.
technical_objections.paper_location: Appendix D, first paragraphs; Application to GW250114; Fig. 3

technical_objections.evidence: Each multipole includes source-dependent amplitude/phase parameters in addition to mass and spin. Appendix D reports significant correlations among phenomenological parameters, and the paper does not show that these degrees of freedom cannot hide beyond-GR deviations or mimic changes in the Kerr reflection amplitude.
technical_objections.objection: The phenomenological nuisance parameters are not sufficiently characterized for a robust GR consistency test.
technical_objections.paper_location: GreyRing section; Appendix B; Appendix D, Fig. 11 discussion

technical_objections.evidence: The paper motivates the reflection amplitude from the Green's-function structure, but the added phase correction is selected by comparing parameterizations and by mismatch reduction; the Laurent-expansion language does not amount to a derivation of the chosen term.
technical_objections.objection: The phase correction is primarily empirical, so the full complex template has weaker theoretical grounding than the greybody-factor motivation alone suggests.
technical_objections.paper_location: Main text Eq. (4); Appendix B, GreyRing model and Fig. 4

technical_objections.evidence: Appendix D reports biased phenomenological parameters at low frequency cutoffs, loss of constraining power at high cutoffs, and a stable intermediate range. This is a real analysis choice even if the mass/spin posteriors appear relatively stable in the example.
technical_objections.objection: The method's claimed freedom from start-time systematics is overstated because the frequency-domain analysis requires a cutoff/window choice that plays an analogous role.
technical_objections.paper_location: Tests of gravity with GreyRing; Appendix D, Figs. 9-10

technical_objections.evidence: The mismatch inner product is explicitly defined without detector-noise weighting, while parameter-estimation indistinguishability criteria and comparisons to detector-facing models usually depend on noise-weighted errors over the analysis band.
technical_objections.objection: The paper uses unweighted mismatch values to support claims about detector-SNR indistinguishability and comparisons with other waveform models.
technical_objections.paper_location: Validation against numerical simulations, Eqs. (5)-(6); Discussion paragraph on SNR reach; comparison to Ref. [52]

technical_objections.evidence: The validation is restricted to comparable-mass, quasicircular, aligned-spin/nonprecessing SXS simulations. Spherical-spheroidal mixing, nonlinearities, precession, and eccentricity are acknowledged as deferred work.
technical_objections.objection: The validation scope is narrower than some headline language suggests.
technical_objections.paper_location: Validation against numerical simulations; Appendix B; Discussion final paragraphs

technical_objections.evidence: The code is described as available upon request, while the main claims depend on fitting choices, waveform processing, sampler settings, detector data handling, and frequency-band choices.
technical_objections.objection: Reproducibility is limited for a numerically driven paper.
